coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic depolymerization of lignin to produce high value-added chemicals by carbon quantum dots modified WO3 nanosheets
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Zhijie
Liu, Xutang
Zhao, Wei
Wang, Fei
Cui, Mingyu
Dong, Linyang
Liang, Chong
Wang, Dingkai
Fu, Shuiyuan
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalysis, a sustainable development strategy, employed to produce value-added chemicals from lignin, has attracted much attention due to its low energy consumption and mild reaction conditions. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and tungsten oxide (WO3) composite photocatalysts (CQDs/WO3) were prepared by ultrasonic assisted wet impregnation method in this study. The CQDs/WO3 nanosheets exhibit good optical properties and satisfactory performance of lignin depolymerization. Characterization results embody that the addition of CQDs significantly changes the morphology of WO3. Compared with bulk pure WO3, CQDs/WO3 becomes a nanosheet structure in the size of ∼ 200 nm. Furthermore, CQDs can absorb photogenerated electrons as well as reduce the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. The strong oxidizing ability of n-type semiconductor WO3 enables it to effectively break the typical bonds like C-O or C-C near polar functional groups in lignin. Due to this ingenious design, dealkaline lignin conversion was up to 34.21% as well as the highest yields of vanillin and vanillic acid in the form of lignin were 24.74 mg/g and 8.02 mg/g in 2 h at room temperature, visible light illumination and air atmosphere, respectively. And optimized CQDs/WO3 also had satisfactory effects on three natural lignin (grass lignin, pine lignin and birch lignin), which proved its universality. Electron spin resonance experiments and mechanism investigation demonstrated that ·O2 - and ·OH play a weighty role in photocatalytic depolymerization of lignin. This study provides a new gambit for converting lignin to high value-added chemicals (vanillin and vanillic acid) over modified WO3.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic depolymerization of lignin to produce high value-added chemicals by carbon quantum dots modified WO3 nanosheets

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalysis, a sustainable development strategy, employed to produce value-added chemicals from lignin, has attracted much attention due to its low energy consumption and mild reaction conditions. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and tungsten oxide (WO3) composite photocatalysts (CQDs/WO3) were prepared by ultrasonic assisted wet impregnation method in this study. The CQDs/WO3 nanosheets exhibit good optical properties and satisfactory performance of lignin depolymerization. Characterization results embody that the addition of CQDs significantly changes the morphology of WO3. Compared with bulk pure WO3, CQDs/WO3 becomes a nanosheet structure in the size of ∼ 200 nm. Furthermore, CQDs can absorb photogenerated electrons as well as reduce the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. The strong oxidizing ability of n-type semiconductor WO3 enables it to effectively break the typical bonds like C-O or C-C near polar functional groups in lignin. Due to this ingenious design, dealkaline lignin conversion was up to 34.21% as well as the highest yields of vanillin and vanillic acid in the form of lignin were 24.74 mg/g and 8.02 mg/g in 2 h at room temperature, visible light illumination and air atmosphere, respectively. And optimized CQDs/WO3 also had satisfactory effects on three natural lignin (grass lignin, pine lignin and birch lignin), which proved its universality. Electron spin resonance experiments and mechanism investigation demonstrated that ·O2 - and ·OH play a weighty role in photocatalytic depolymerization of lignin. This study provides a new gambit for converting lignin to high value-added chemicals (vanillin and vanillic acid) over modified WO3.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: This study provides a strategy for efficient conversion of lignin to high value-added chemicals using a photocatalyst synthesized by a simple method.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Clean energy resource like alcohols from biomass has attracted more and more attention [1,2] with the depletion of fossil fuels and increasingly severe environmental problems. Lignin has antioxidant, antibacterial activity and strong stability [3,4], composing of three precursors (p-coumarol, sinapyl alcohol, coniferyl alcohol) [5], which is considered to be an ideal raw material for the production of phenolic compounds [6,7]. The unique properties determine that lignin is of difficulty to be processed and utilized, otherwise lignin could be possibly regarded as waste [8]. At present, lignin treatment mainly focuses on pyrolysis [9–11], oxidative [12,13] and reductive depolymerization [14,15], etc. However, most of the research strategies demand harsh conditions, for instance, high temperature, high pressure as well as relatively strong acidic or basic environments. Therefore, it is urgent to work out a way to convert lignin into high value-added chemicals under mild conditions [16].

Photocatalysis has caused much concern for the reason of their mild reaction conditions, low energy consumption, adjustable redox capacity, and high selectivity [17,18]. Nowadays, photocatalysis is widely applied in hydrogen production, CO2 conversion, pollutant degradation, biomass conversion and other fields [19–22]. Photocatalytic depolymerization of lignin has been greatly developed since Kobayakawa [23] first applied TiO2 to lignin depolymerization in 1989, and it was completely decomposed into CO2 and H2O because of the dehydrogenative dimerization tendency of aromatic compounds and the strong oxidation ability of photogenerated holes. Wu [24] applied CdS quantum dots to depolymerize natural birch lignin, and 27 wt% aromatic compounds with ketones were obtained as the main product. Whereas, high productive aromatic monomers were not achieved. Vanillin, as one of the lignin depolymerization products, can be directly used in food, beverage, medicine, cosmetics, as well as preservatives [25]. Srisasiwimon [26] prepared TiO2/lignin-based carbon composited photocatalysts to overcome the high photogenerated electron-hole recombination and photo-corrosion of single catalyst. The conversion of alkali lignin reached 40.28%, and the yield of vanillin was about 1.68%. TiO2 can merely respond to ultraviolet light because of its wide band gap (3.2 eV), accordingly it is necessary to develop visible light responsive catalysts to expand its application fields. Dai [27] constructed CdS/BiOI heterostructure, which could convert sodium lignosulfonate to vanillin with the yield of 10.95 mg/g lignin under visible light. Although aromatic monomers such as vanillin could be obtained, the yield of which was still low. Therefore, improving the yield of vanillin is an urgent problem to be solved.

WO3 is an ideal semiconductor photocatalyst with its narrow band gap, visible light responsibility, high photo-corrosion resistance and ideal stability. Hereby, it is widely used in the field of photocatalytic degradation of pollutants and hydrogen production [28–30]. Zhou [31] fabricated a single-junction p-Si/WO3 photocathode, which can provide a photovoltage of up to 500 mV and effectively overcome the water reduction overpotential. Moreover, catalysts such as 2D/2D WO3/g-C3N4 step-scheme [32] and S-scheme 3D ZnIn2S4/WO3 heterostructure [33] were constructed to further promote the hydrogen evolution performance of the catalyst. WO3/GO/NCQDs [34], GQDs/W18O49/WO3 homojunction [34] and WO3/Bi2WO6 [35] etc. have been reported for pollutant degradation. It can be seen that WO3 has a satisfactory catalytic effect in photocatalytic hydrogen production and degradation of pollutants. However, WO3 is rarely used in the field of photocatalytic conversion of biomass. Liu [36] prepared a series of WO3/g-C3N4/BN to fulfill photocatalytic pre-oxidation-ultrasonic cavitation under visible light, whose total yield of phenolic monomers was around 1.92 wt%. This verifies that WO3 has great potential in the field of photocatalytic depolymerization of lignin.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can be used as electronic media. It also has unique up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) properties and good photogenerated electron-hole separation ability [37]. CQDs/TiO2 has been reported to be applied to photocatalytic degradation of lignin [38]. Combining CQDs with WO3 to form CQDs/WO3 photocatalyst can effectively promote carrier transfer and reduce photogenerated electron-hole recombination rate. It is also a novel finesse to apply it to photocatalytic depolymerization of lignin.

In this work, CQDs/WO3 nanosheet photocatalysts were prepared by ultrasonic wet assisted impregnation method. And the photocatalytic depolymerization of lignin to produce high value-added chemicals over CQDs/WO3 was investigated to evaluate its performance and recyclability.

Sodium tungsten oxide dihydrate (Na2WO4·2 H2O), 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO), silver nitrate (AgNO3) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na), isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and acetonitrile (CH3CN) were supplied from Xilong chemical Co., Ltd. Hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), hydrochloric acid (HCl), 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO), and 1,4-Benzoquinone (PBQ) were obtained from Aladdin reagent Co., Ltd. Citric acid monohydrate (C6H10O8) and nitric acid (HNO3) were purchased from Sinopharm chemical reagent Co., Ltd. Vanillin (98%) and vanillic acid (98%) were obtained from Energy Chemical Co., Ltd. Lignin (dealkaline) (marked as lignin) (CAS:9005–53–2) was supplied by Tokyo chemical industry (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. All the chemicals adopted in this work are of analytical grades (AR) without further purification.

WO3 nanosheets were prepared by sol-gel method [32]. Generally, 3.0 g Na2WO4·2 H2O was dissolved in 10.0 mL deionized water. Subsequently, 10.0 mL nitric acid solution (1.6 mol/L) was slowly added to the as-mentioned aqueous solution under magnetic stirring at room temperature and maintained for 6 h. The light-yellow solid was collected by centrifugation, washed with deionized water/ethanol, and raised to 550 °C at a heating rate of 8 °C/min in a muffle furnace for 6 h.

CQDs were prepared by the pyrolysis method [37,39]. Typically, 2.0 g citric acid was added into the porcelain boat, the temperature was raised to 200 °C at a heating rate of 5 °C/min in a muffle furnace and kept for 1 h. The obtained product was dissolved in 1 mol/L NaOH solution, and the pH of which was adjusted to 7 with hydrochloric acid to acquire CQDs solution.

A series of CQDs/WO3 were prepared by ultrasonic assisted wet impregnation method ( Scheme 1) [40]. In general, 0.5 g WO3 was added ultrasonically to 10.0 mL, 20.0 mL, and 30.0 mL CQDs solution for 1 h, respectively. Subsequently, the solution was stirred at room temperature for 24 h, centrifuged, washed with deionized water/ethanol, and dried at 60 °C for 6 h to gain CQDs/WO3, which were marked as 10CQDs/WO3, 20CQDs/WO3, 30CQDs/WO3 according to the amount of CQDs added. General characterization of catalysts is presented in SI-1.

The standard procedure for photocatalytic depolymerization of lignin is shown in SI-2, and the basic information of the standard curve is shown in Figure S1 and Table S1. Three preparation methods of natural lignin can be found in SI-3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and morphology of the photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1a displays the XRD patterns for pure WO3 and CQDs/WO3 composites with different amounts of CQDs. Based on analysis, the formation of monoclinic WO3 have been confirmed by JCPDS No. 43–1035. For WO3, the lattice planes matched by the diffraction peak is shown in Table. S2. The diffraction peak of CQDs/WO3 is almost the same as that of pure WO3. It is notable that the (020) diffraction peak shifted slightly to the high angle, which is ascribed to the interaction between WO3 and CQDs causing the lattice spacing narrower [41]. The outcome proves that CQDs and WO3 form a stable structure.

Fig. 1b renders the FTIR spectra of CQDs, WO3, and CQDs/WO3, respectively. It is worth noting that all spectra have a broad band in the range of 3600–3200 cm−1, which is the O-H stretching vibration of physically adsorbed water [42]. For CQDs, the characteristic peaks at 1380 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1 are attributed to the in-plane bending vibration of -OH and the stretching vibration of CO bond, respectively [38]. The characteristic peaks at 2850 and 2930 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of CH3 and CH2. For pure WO3, the band in the range of 910–660 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of W-O bond [43]. A wide infrared absorption peak appears, confirming the formation of WO3 nanosheets due to the nanometer size effect and interface effect of the nanoparticles [44]. The characteristic peaks of CQDs and WO3 are basically preserved in the FTIR spectra of CQDs/WO3. Furthermore, with the increase of CQDs addition, the characteristic peak width of WO3 gradually narrowed, purporting that CQDs caused the modification of WO3.

Fig. 2 presents the morphology and microstructure of WO3 and 20CQDs/WO3. As can be seen from Fig. 2a, the SEM image of WO3 exhibits an irregular block morphology. After adding CQDs, the morphology changed significantly (Figs. 2b and 2c). The SEM image of 20CQDs/WO3 displays the morphology of nanoflakes with a size of ∼ 200 nm. The TEM image of 20CQDs/WO3 (Fig. 2d) presents a layered stacking structure, the black main part is WO3 and the edge part is CQDs. The HRTEM of Fig. 2e is also obtained by a partial scanning in Fig. 2d, and the lattice fringes of CQDs and WO3 can be clearly seen. The lattice spacing of 0.289 nm corresponds to (002) crystal plane of CQDs, while the lattice spacing of 0.311 and 0.379 nm are (112) and (002) crystal planes of WO3 [45]. The above evidences are corresponded to the XRD spectra. Furthermore, the EDS spectrum of 20CQDs is exhibited in Fig. 2f and Fig. S2. Existence of C, O and W elements indicated that CQDs and WO3 composite photocatalysts were successfully prepared.

Fig. 3 shows the XPS spectra of pure WO3 and 20CQDs/WO3 nanosheets. The full-scale XPS spectra (Fig. 3a) confirmed the presence of C, O, and W element in the corresponding materials. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s is exhibited in Fig. 3b. The characteristic peaks at the binding energy of 284.6 eV and 285.0 eV correspond to the C-C and C-O/CO bond of CQDs, respectively. They are confirmed to be the oxygen-containing functional group of CQDs [46]. The O1s spectra of WO3 are displayed in Fig. 3c. The characteristic peaks at the binding energy of 530.5 eV, 531.3 eV and 532.5 eV correspond to the lattice oxygen atoms, adsorbed oxygen and adsorbed water in WO3, respectively [47]. The binding energy of lattice oxygen atoms of 20CQDs/WO3 shifts to higher binding energy, implying a strong interaction between CQDs and WO3, which establishes electron transport channels to facilitate the flow of electrons to the CQDs. Furthermore, the peak intensity of the adsorbed oxygen increases, declaring that more oxygen is adsorbed on its surface, which indirectly proves that it has a larger specific surface area [47]. Fig. 3d is the W4f spectra of WO3 and 20CQDs/WO3. The spin-orbit peaks at binding energies of 41.5 eV, 37.8 eV and 35.6 eV are attributed to W5p3/2, W4f5/2 and W4f7/2 of W6+, respectively. In the 20CQDs/WO3 spectrum, the spin-orbit peak of W6+ shifts to higher binding energy [32]. The above testimonies authenticate that CQDs and WO3 form a stable structure.

The UV-Vis DRS, PL, EIS spectra and transient photocurrent response of different photocatalysts are manifested in Fig. 4. It is not hard to see in Fig. 4a that the light absorption edge of WO3 is about 479.4 nm. After the addition of CQDs, the light absorption edge redshifts. For instance, the optical absorption edge of 20CQDs/WO3 is 510.3 nm. It is mainly attributed to the unique UCPL property of CQDs, which can absorb long-wavelength light and emit short-wavelength light [37,48]. Furthermore, the PL spectra was used to determine recombination rate of electron-hole pairs [49]. Fig. 4b demonstrates the PL spectra of different photocatalysts at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, and the emission range is between 690 and 710 nm with the main peak center of about 700 nm. As we can see, the PL intensity decreased significantly by incorporating CQDs into WO3. Especially, 20CQDs/WO3 has the enhanced ability to suppress the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs due to its weakest PL intensity. The migration and separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers were further evaluated by EIS [50]. The radius of the arc in EIS corresponds to the internal resistance of the material. It can be seen from Fig. 4c that 20CQDs/WO3 has the smallest arc radius, which reveals the smallest resistance and fastest carrier migration [51]. It can be seen from the transient photocurrent response spectrum that the photocurrent of pure WO3 is ∼0.9 μA/cm2. After the addition of CQDs, the photocurrent of the composite catalyst increased significantly, the photocurrent of 10CQDs/WO3 and 30CQDs/WO3 could reach ∼1.2 μA/cm2. In particular, the photocurrent of 20CQDs/WO3 could reach ∼1.4 μA/cm2, which is 1.5 times that of pure WO3. It has good separation ability of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, which is consistent with the results exhibited in PL and EIS spectra [52]. The wider light absorption range of 20CQDs/WO3 gives it a higher light absorption efficiency. The high photogenerated carrier mobility and separation rate enable the production of more reactive species (·O2 - and ·OH), which further promote lignin conversion.

The energy band structure of the photocatalysts is presented in Fig. 5. The band gap energy (E
g ) can be calculated from the UV-Vis DRS (Fig. 5a), and the formula is as follows [53]:

where h is Planck's constant, υ is the frequency of the photon, α is the absorption coefficient and A is the constant. The n value depends on the nature of the electronic transition, for the direct band gap, n = 1/2, and for indirect band gap, n = 2. WO3 is an indirect band gap semiconductor with n value of 2. It is not hard to see that the band gap of pure WO3 is 2.61 eV. After adding CQDs, the band gap becomes narrower with the narrowest band gap 2.59 eV of 20CQDs/WO3. The phenomenon is attributed to the UCPL property of CQDs, which expands the light absorption range and reduces the band gap energy. The flat band potential (E
fb ) of semiconductor can be calculated by Mott-Schottky curve, and then the conduction band potential (E
CB ) can be estimated [43]. Fig. 5b describes the Mott-Schottky curves of different photocatalysts. The E

The computing outputs are listed in Table. S3. The addition of CQDs forms a new valence band through an energy-favorable reaction, further reducing the band gap [34].Compared with other photocatalysts, 20CQDs/WO3 has lower conduction band, lower valence band and narrower band gap. The demonstrations explain that it has stronger oxidation ability and a wider range of light response, which can better break the typical bonds in lignin. In order to confirm the accuracy of the calculated band structure, the E VB value of pure WO3 was measured by VB-XPS. As shown in Fig. S3, the E VB, XPS of 20CQDs/WO3 is 2.67 eV, and the E VB, NHE can be obtained by the corresponding calculation. The calculation formula is as follows [58]:

where φ is the instrument work function (4.5 eV), and the calculated E
VB , NHE value is 2.61 eV, which is consistent with the computation of the formula (3).

A series of CQDs/WO3 photocatalysts are implemented for photocatalytic depolymerization of lignin under different conditions. The products were analyzed by GC-MS. The total ion chromatogram and product distribution of lignin depolymerization products are shown in Fig. S4 and Table. S4, respectively. It is not hard to see that the products are all monocyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, among which the relative contents of vanillin and vanillic acid are the highest. The high catalytic efficiency of the catalyst without destroying the benzene ring structure is an important condition for the production of high value-added chemicals. Accordingly, the yield of vanillin and vanillic acid is used as the evaluation standard, whose types and contents were detected by HPLC. The representative HPLC chromatograms are listed in Fig. S5.

Fig. 6a demonstrates the effect of reaction time on the yield of product. The yield reached more than 30 mg/g lignin after 1.5 h depolymerization. And the highest yield was reached at 32.76 mg/g lignin in the duration of 2 h. Prolonging the reaction time, the yield decreased, which implicates that the product will be further converted over time [59]. Therefore, the appropriate reaction time is 2 h.

The effect of different solvent ratios (CH3CN/H2O) on the depolymerization are given in Fig. 6b. The results conducted that under the conditions of pure water or acetonitrile, the yields of the products were only 20.91 mg/g lignin and 19.56 mg/g lignin, respectively. With the increase of CH3CN ratio, the yield of the product increased. When the solvent ratio was 3/2, the highest yield of the product reached 32.76 mg/g lignin. As the ratio of CH3CN continued to increase, the total yield of the product decreased. The main reason is that H2O can react with h + to generate ·OH, which is an indispensable radical intermediate in the reaction [60].

Fig. 6c presents the effect of different reaction atmosphere. The total yield was the lowest under nitrogen condition, presenting 23.18 mg/g lignin. The main reason was that the lack of oxygen made it difficult to generate ·O2 -, which was one of the main radical intermediates in the oxidation reaction, thus limiting the reaction [57]. The total yield under oxygen condition was 24.61 mg/g lignin, which may be caused by the further oxidation of the product. In the air atmosphere, not only oxygen is provided to generate ·O2 -, but also the appropriate oxygen concentration reduces the mineralization of the product. Hereby, the product yield reached 32.76 mg/g lignin.

Fig. 6d manifests the influence of different photocatalysts under the premise of other conditions unchanged. It can be seen from the diagram that without photocatalyst, the yield is 17.59 mg/g lignin. It reflects that lignin can be partially depolymerized when dissolved in water. And the yield of product reached 24.31 mg/g lignin after the addition of WO3. When combining CQDs, the yield of the product increased, especially when the amount of CQDs is 20 mL, the total yield of the product reached 32.76 mg/g lignin. Whereas, lignin did not react further under dark conditions. Excessive CQDs will be enriched on the surface of the WO3 to cause agglomeration, which affects the light absorption and the yield reduced accordingly [61].

Lignin conversion was calculated according to the formula [62]:
(5)
Conversion = ( 1 −
Quality of lignin residue
Amount of lignin input
) × 100 %

The lignin conversion under suitable conditions was as high as 34.21%. Therefore, the photocatalyst 20CQDs/WO3 exhibits a good photocatalytic activity in the depolymerization of lignin, and achieves a considerable photocatalytic efficiency of 12.37 mg glignin −1 h−1 than other publications (Table. S5) under visible light.

Fig. 7a depicts the reusability of 20CQDs/WO3 under suitable conditions. The total yield of the product can still reach 26.28 mg/g lignin even after six cycles, which represents that the photocatalyst has good stability. Fig. 7b exhibits the XRD spectrum of the photocatalyst after use, which is in consistence with the fresh photocatalyst. It illustrates that the catalyst has no obvious change in chemical properties during photocatalytic reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of lignin and its photocatalytic products
ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic experiments were carried out on dealkaline lignin and other three natural lignin simples (grass lignin, pine lignin and birch lignin) to further explore the universal applicability of the catalyst. And the changes of lignin before and after the reaction were analyzed by GPC and FTIR.

GPC analysis was performed on the raw lignin and products to further determine the molecular weight changes of lignin before and after the reaction. As for dealkaline lignin ( Fig. 8a), the raw lignin only has a macromolecular peak at 4 min. However, several small molecule peaks appeared after 4 min in the GPC chromatogram of the product, which confirmed that the macromolecular structure of lignin was obviously broken after the reaction [63]. In addition, the analysis of GPC chromatograms (Fig. S6) of three natural lignin simples also revealed the same results. Moreover, the average molecular weight distribution of the four lignin simples were presented in Table S6. The products have lower Mw and PDI compared with the raw lignin simples, which signifies that the products have smaller molecular weight and narrower molecular weight distribution [64]. Obviously, the depolymerization of lignin macromolecular fragments is promoted by the photocatalytic reaction. The consequence was consistent with those of GC/MS.

FTIR spectra (Fig. 8b) exhibits that the characteristic peak intensity of the products reduces compared with the raw dealkaline lignin. Table S7 enumerations the attribution of different absorption peaks. Some characteristic peaks of lignin vanished after the reaction, manifesting that some linkages in lignin were cleaved. The intensity of the absorption peak at 1137 cm−1 significantly reduced, which was ascribed to the obvious cleavage of C-O bond. The characteristic peak caused by the benzene ring skeleton vibration at 1600 cm−1 did not shift. And a new characteristic peak appears at 1665 cm−1, which is attributed to the stretching vibration of the C-O bond in ketone, explaining the formation of aromatic monomers. Similarly, in the FTIR spectra (Fig. S7) of the three natural lignin simples before and after the reaction. The intensity and number of absorption peaks decreased significantly, and new absorption peaks generated after the reaction. The characteristic peaks of C-O bonds in the three lignin samples significantly reduced after the reaction without the benzene ring skeleton change. The verifications represent that 20CQDs/WO3 exhibits satisfactory selectivity.

The possible reaction mechanism is proposed ( Scheme 2) according to the characterization of the photocatalyst and the analysis of the photocatalytic reaction. The reaction process is as follows [65]:

WO3 absorbs photons under illumination to generate photogenerated electrons and holes. The photogenerated electrons are absorbed by CQDs, so that the photogenerated electron-hole pairs are spatially separated [34]. After that, CQDs release electrons. e - reacts with O2 to form ·O2 -, and h
+ reacts with H2O to form ·OH. ·O2 -, ·OH and H+ participated in the lignin depolymerization reaction together to generate the corresponding products.

In order to explore the mechanism of photocatalytic depolymerization of lignin, different radical sacrificial agents were added for several experiments. The reaction effect is displayed in Fig. 9a. Among them, IPA, PBQ, EDTA-2Na and AgNO3 are ·OH, ·O2 -, h + and e - sacrifice agents, respectively. It can be clearly seen from the diagram that after the addition of radical sacrificial agent, the photocatalytic depolymerization of lignin reaction has been significantly inhibited. The addition of IPA to the reaction system gave birth to the lowest yield of 25.58 mg/g lignin. When PBQ was added to the system, the total yield of the product decreased to 26.05 mg/g lignin. The yield of the product (27.62 mg/g lignin) decreased after the addition of EDTA-2Na, which affected the generation of ·OH. e - is an important active substance to produce ·O2 -. After the addition of AgNO3, the yield of the product decreased significantly, which was only 26.09 mg/g lignin. The ESR signals of ·OH and ·O2 - were studied by using DMPO as a free radical scavenger (Fig. 9b) to further explore the active substances in the photocatalytic system. Under the condition of no light irradiation, no ESR signal generated. After 10 min of illumination, the ESR signals of DMPO- ·OH and DMPO- ·O2 - generated, and DMPO- ·OH was stronger than DMPO- ·O2 -. The verifications are corresponded to Fig. 9a.

CQDs/WO3 nanosheets prepared by ultrasonic assisted wet impregnation method was investigated systematically in this paper. The addition of CQDs changed the morphology and optical properties of WO3, making it have prominent photogenerated electron-hole separation ability, satisfactory carrier migration rate, and broadening light absorption range. CQDs/WO3 has a good effect in the field of photocatalytic lignin conversion. This study confirmed that the total yield of vanillin and vanillic acid could reach 32.76 mg/g lignin under visible light and a short reaction interval within 2 h. In addition, 20CQDs/WO3 also behaved stable separability, and the yield of the product could still reach 80.02% for the first time after six cycles. This study is expected to provide new ideas for the direction of photocatalytic depolymerization of lignin to generate value-added chemicals.
